content: A motor driving a solid circular steel shaft transmits $$40$$ $$kW$$ of power at $$500$$ $$rpm$$. If the diameter of the shaft is $$40$$ $$mm,$$ the maximum shear stress in the shaft is ________ $$MP$$a.

The cross sections of two hollow bars made of the same material are concentric circles as shown in the figure. It is given that 𝑟3 > 𝑟1 and 𝑟4 > 𝑟2 , and that the areas of the cross-sections are the same. J1 and J2 are the torsional rigidities of the bars on the left and right, respectively. The ratioJ2/J1 is
Consider a stepped shaft subjected to a twisting moment applied at $$B$$ as shown in the figure. Assume shear modulus, $$G=77$$ GPa. The angle of twist at $$C$$ (in degrees) is ________

A rope brake dynamometer attached to the crank shaft of an $$I.C.$$ engine measures a brake power of $$10$$ kW when the speed of rotation of the shaft is $$400$$ rad/s. The shaft torque (in N-m) sensed by the dynamometer is ________

Two solid circular shafts of $${{R_1}}$$ and $${{R_2}}$$ are subjected to same torque. The maximum shear stresses developed in the two shafts are $${\tau _1}$$ and $${\tau _2}.$$   If $${{{R_1}} \over {{R_2}}} = 2,$$  then $${{{\tau _1}} \over {{\tau _2}}}$$ is _______.

A solid circular shaft of diameter $$d$$ is subjected to a combined bending moment, $$M$$ and torque, $$T.$$ The material property to be used for designing the shaft using the relation $${{16T} \over {\pi {d^3}}}\sqrt {{M^2} + {T^2}} $$
For a circular shaft of diameter $$'d'$$ subjected to torque $$T,$$ the maximum value of the shear stress is

Maximum shear stress developed on the surface of a solid circular shaft under pure torsion is 240 MPa. If the shaft diameter is doubled then the maximum shear stress developed corresponding to the same torque will be

A cylindrical transmission shaft of length 1.5 m and diameter 100 mm is made of a linear elastic material with a shear modulus of 80 GPa. While operating at 500 rpm, the angle of twist across its length is found to be 0.5 degrees.

The power transmitted by the shaft at this speed is _______kW. (Rounded off to two decimal places)

Take π = 3.14.
explanation:
The Torsion Equation for a shaft is given by:

$ \frac{T}{J} = \frac{τ}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L} $

Where:

  • $T$ is the torque,

  • $J$ is the polar moment of inertia,

  • $τ$ is the shear stress,

  • $r$ is the radius of the shaft,

  • $G$ is the shear modulus,

  • $ \theta $ is the angle of twist,

  • $L$ is the length of the shaft.

The power $P$ transmitted by any solid shaft is given by:

$ P = \text{Torque} \ (T) \times \text{Angular velocity} \ (\omega) $

Where the angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$ \omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} $

with $N$ being the speed in rpm.

Given :

  • $N = 500$ rpm

  • $G = 80$ GPa

  • Diameter $d = 100$ mm

  • Length $L = 1.5$ m

  • Angle of twist $ \theta = 0.5^\circ $

Using the equation:

$ \theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} $

Substitute the given values to find Torque $T$:

$ T = \frac{G J \theta}{L} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times \frac{\pi}{32} \times (100)^4 \times 0.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180}}{1500} $

Calculating step-by-step:

  • Convert diameter to radius:

$ r = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ mm} $

  • Calculate the polar moment of inertia $J$:

$ J = \frac{\pi r^4}{2} = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{2} $

Substitute $J$ to find $T$:

$ T = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times J \times \theta}{L} $

$ J = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{2} $

$ T = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times \frac{\pi}{32} \times (100)^4 \times 0.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180}}{1500} $

This simplifies to:

$ T = 4569261.297 \text{ N-mm} $

$ T = 4569.261 \text{ N-m} $

Finally, calculate the power $P$:

$ P = \frac{2 \pi NT}{60} $

$ P = \frac{2 \pi \times 500 \times 4569.261}{60} $

$ P = 239245.96 \text{ W} $

$ P = 239.246 \text{ kW} $

content: A shaft of length L is made of two materials, one in the inner core and the other in the outer rim, and the two are perfectly joined together (no slip at the interface) along the entire length of the shaft. The diameter of the inner core is d; and the external diameter of the rim is d0, as shown in the figure. The modulus of rigidity of the core and rim materials are Gi and G0, respectively. It is given that d0 = 2di and Gi = 3G0. When the shaft is twisted by application of a torque along the shaft axis, the maximum shear stress developed in the outer rim and the inner core turn out to be τ0 and τi, respectively. All the deformations are in the elastic range and stress-strain relations are linear. Then the ratio τi/τ0 is (round off to 2 decimal places).
explanation:
Given equations :

$ \begin{aligned} d_0 &= 2d_i \\ G_i &= 3G_0 \end{aligned} $

Since the core and rim are perfectly joined, we have :

$ L = L = L \quad \text{and} \quad \theta = \theta_i = \theta_0 $

From the torsion equation :

$ \frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L} $

Maximum shear stress is given by :

$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{G \theta r_{\max}}{L} \quad \text{...(i)} $

For the core :

$ \begin{aligned} \tau_i &= \frac{G_i \theta_i d_i}{2L} \quad \text{...(ii)} \end{aligned} $

For the outer rim :

$ \begin{aligned} \tau_0 &= \frac{G_0 \theta_0 d_0}{2L} \quad \text{...(iii)} \end{aligned} $

From equations (ii) and (iii) :

$ \frac{\tau_i}{\tau_0} = \frac{\frac{G_i \theta_i d_i}{2L}}{\frac{G_0 \theta_0 d_0}{2L}} $

Simplifying further :

$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\tau_i}{\tau_0} &= \frac{G_i \theta_i d_i}{2L} \frac{2L}{G_0 \theta_0 d_0} \\\\ &= \frac{3G_0 \times \theta_i \times d}{2L} \frac{2L}{G_0 \times \theta_0 \times 2d} \\\\ &= \frac{3 \times 2}{2 \times 2} \\\\ &= \frac{6}{4} \\\\ &= 1.5 \end{aligned} $

Thus, the ratio $ \frac{\tau_i}{\tau_0} $ is $ 1.5 $.
